PixiJS V5 Tutorial (37) - Adding stats.js to Watch Your FPS

Let's talk about stats.js. And while we're at it, I'm going to switch to a different demo to use as our reference — the treasure hunter we built earlier doesn't really push the performance hard enough, so you can't actually see anything interesting happening.
For the example here, I'll be using this pen:
See the Pen PIXI dat.GUI by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

The reason I picked this particular pen is that I'd already wired up dat.GUI, which makes it nice and easy to tweak things on the fly. So I'll just drop Stats.js straight in — I won't walk through that setup again, since we've covered it before.
The one thing I'm changing is removing a piece of code on purpose, to deliberately create some lag. It's this part:
all the way up. If your computer isn't exactly a beast, you should pretty quickly start seeing the FPS tank and your fan spinning like crazy.
See the Pen PIXI dat.GUI + Stat.js by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
(Inner voice: my machine's a bit too good, so honestly I can barely feel it QQ)
Basically stats.js has four modes you can switch between. If you were paying attention in the earlier chapters, the clever you out there probably already noticed this line:
The slightly annoying part is that you can only show one of these four at a time. The

FPS Frames rendered in the last second. The higher the number the better.
MS Milliseconds needed to render a frame. The lower the number the better.
MB MBytes of allocated memory. (Run Chrome with --enable-precise-memory-info)
CUSTOM User-defined panel support.
(frames per second): how many frames got rendered in the last second. Higher is better — for Pixi I'd aim for 60.
(milliseconds): how long it takes to render a frame. Lower is better.
(memory): how much memory has been allocated. This needs Chrome with
— if you're on a browser other than Chrome you won't be able to see it.
: honestly not sure about this one — no idea what you'd customize here.
